::: proof

Adapted from [@klein2003survival, §8.3, Theoretical Note 1, p. 257].

Assume that the censoring is noninformative
(conditional on $\vx$, censoring times are independent of event times)
and that there are no tied event times.
For subject $j$, let $T_j$ be the event time, $C_j$ the censoring time,
$Y_j = \min(T_j, C_j)$ the observed follow-up time, and
$D_j = \1{T_j \le C_j}$ the event indicator (exit reason),
matching the $Y$, $D$ notation of the
[censored-data likelihood](intro-to-survival-analysis.qmd#sec-likelihood-with-censoring).
Let $R(t) = \{j : Y_j \ge t\}$ be the risk set at $t$
(the [risk set definition](intro-to-survival-analysis.qmd#def-risk-set)),
let $t_1 < \dots < t_K$ be the $K$ distinct observed failure times, and
let $(i)$ denote the subject who fails at $t_i$
(unique under the no-ties assumption).

**The full joint likelihood of the observed data.**
The observed data set is $\{(Y_j, D_j, \vx_j)\}_{j=1}^n$.
As derived for the
[censored-data likelihood](intro-to-survival-analysis.qmd#sec-likelihood-with-censoring),
a subject observed to fail ($D_j = 1$) contributes the event-time
density $\haz(Y_j \mid \vx_j)\,\surv(Y_j \mid \vx_j)$, while a
subject still censored ($D_j = 0$) contributes the survival
probability $\surv(Y_j \mid \vx_j)$; under noninformative
censoring the two cases combine into the single contribution
$\surv(Y_j \mid \vx_j)\,\haz(Y_j \mid \vx_j)^{D_j}$.
Subjects are independent, so the full likelihood of the entire observed
data set is the product

$$
\Lik(\b, \haz_0)
= \prod_{j=1}^{n}
\surv(Y_j \mid \vx_j)\,
\haz(Y_j \mid \vx_j)^{D_j}.
$$ {#eq-ph-obs-lik}

No risk set appears yet: @eq-ph-obs-lik conditions on nothing but the
covariates. The rest of the proof rewrites it as a product over short
time intervals, regroups those factors by interval to make the risk
sets emerge, and only then decomposes each event's factor into the
partial likelihood and a discarded remainder.

**Discretize the survival factors over a time grid.**
Partition $[0, \max_j Y_j]$ into intervals of width $\Delta t$
with left endpoints $\tau$.
For each subject $j$ and grid point $\tau$, define the
**interval failure probability**

$$
\ba
q_j(\tau)
&\eqdef \haz(\tau \mid \vx_j)\,\Delta t
\\
&= \blue{\haz_0(\tau)\,\Delta t}\;\red{\hazfactor(\vx_j)},
\ea
$$ {#eq-ph-interval-fail-prob}

the first-order probability that subject $j$ fails during the
length-$\Delta t$ interval at $\tau$, given that it is still at risk at
$\tau$;
the second equality is the hazard decomposition (@thm-ph-haz-decomp).
To track how this piece fits into the larger products, we color the
$\blue{\text{baseline-hazard factor }\haz_0(\cdot)\,\Delta t}$ — shared
by everyone at risk at a given time, and ultimately discarded — and the
$\red{\text{subject-specific hazard ratio }\hazfactor(\vx)}$ — the focal
quantity the partial likelihood isolates.
Correspondingly, $1 - q_j(\tau)$ is, to first order, the probability
that $j$ *survives* that interval.
We treat $q_j(\tau)$ as the basic building block from here on:
every factor below is a product of $q_j(\tau)$'s and $(1 - q_j(\tau))$'s,
and we manipulate these pieces directly instead of re-expanding the
hazard at each step.

Writing the survival function as the exponential of the negative
cumulative hazard,
$\surv(t \mid \vx) = \expf{-\int_0^{t} \haz(u \mid \vx)\,du}$
(see the [survival/cumulative hazard relationship](intro-to-survival-analysis.qmd#cor-surv-int-haz)),
and approximating the integral by its left Riemann sum over the grid,

$$
\ba
\surv(Y_j \mid \vx_j)
&= \expf{-\!\!\sum_{\tau < Y_j} q_j(\tau) + o(1)}
\\
&= \prod_{\tau < Y_j} \expf{-q_j(\tau)}
\\
&\approx \prod_{\tau < Y_j}\paren{1 - q_j(\tau)}.
\ea
$$ {#eq-ph-surv-discretized}

When $D_j = 1$, the same building block describes the final interval:
$q_j(Y_j) = \haz(Y_j \mid \vx_j)\,\Delta t \approx \Pr\sb{j \text{ fails in } [Y_j,\, Y_j + \Delta t) \mid j \in R(Y_j)}$,
the first-order probability that $j$ fails in its final interval.
Substituting @eq-ph-surv-discretized into @eq-ph-obs-lik
(and absorbing the constant $\Delta t^{D_j}$, which does not depend
on $\b$ or $\haz_0$),

$$
\Lik(\b, \haz_0)
\approx
\prod_{j=1}^{n}
\sb{
q_j(Y_j)^{D_j}
\prod_{\tau < Y_j}\paren{1 - q_j(\tau)}
}.
$$ {#eq-ph-lik-by-subject}

**Regroup the factors by interval — the risk sets emerge.**
Each factor in @eq-ph-lik-by-subject is indexed by a (subject, interval)
pair: subject $j$ contributes one factor to every interval $\tau$ in
which it is still at risk, i.e. every $\tau$ with $j \in R(\tau)$.
Grouping the double product by interval instead, interval $\tau$ collects one
factor from every member $m \in R(\tau)$ — a failure factor for the one
subject (if any) who fails in $\tau$, and a survival factor for each
subject who does not:

$$
\Lik(\b, \haz_0)
\approx
\prod_{\tau} \Lik_\tau,
\qquad
\Lik_\tau
= \prod_{m \in R(\tau)}
\begin{cases}
q_m(\tau), & m \text{ fails in interval } \tau,\\[2pt]
1 - q_m(\tau), & \text{otherwise.}
\end{cases}
$$ {#eq-ph-lik-by-interval}

By construction $\Lik_\tau$ is the joint probability, *given the risk set
$R(\tau)$*, of the outcomes observed in interval $\tau$. Under the
no-ties assumption an interval holds at most one failure, so the
intervals split into two kinds.

For an interval $\tau$ with no failure, every at-risk subject survives it:

$$
\Lik_\tau^{\text{no failure}}
= \prod_{m \in R(\tau)} \paren{1 - q_m(\tau)}.
$$ {#eq-ph-nofail-interval}

For the interval containing event time $t_i$, subject $(i)$ fails and
every other member of $R(t_i)$ survives:

$$
\Lik_{t_i}^{\text{failure}}
= q_{(i)}(t_i)
\prod_{m \in R(t_i),\, m \ne (i)} \paren{1 - q_m(t_i)}.
$$ {#eq-ph-fail-interval}

**A single subject's failure probability.**
To evaluate @eq-ph-fail-interval we need the per-subject failure
probability inside a risk set.
For any $j \in R(t_i)$, since $j \in R(t_i)$ means
$Y_j = \min(T_j,\,C_j) \ge t_i$, we have both $T_j \ge t_i$ and
$C_j \ge t_i$; under noninformative censoring the event $C_j \ge t_i$
carries no information about $T_j$ given $\vx_j$, so conditioning on
$j \in R(t_i)$ is equivalent to conditioning on $T_j \ge t_i$ alone.
By the [hazard definition](intro-to-survival-analysis.qmd#def-hazard),
this conditional failure probability is, to first order, the building
block @eq-ph-interval-fail-prob evaluated at $t_i$:

$$
\ba
\Pr\sb{j \text{ fails at } t_i \mid j \in R(t_i)}
&\approx q_j(t_i)
\\
&= \blue{\haz_0(t_i)\,\Delta t}\;\red{\hazfactor(\vx_j)},
\ea
$$ {#eq-ph-single-subj}

with the $\blue{\haz_0(t_i)\,\Delta t}$ factor shared by every subject in
the risk set and only the $\red{\hazfactor(\vx_j)}$ factor varying with
$j$.
In particular the survival factors in @eq-ph-fail-interval are each
$1 - O(\Delta t)$, so to first order

$$
\ba
\Lik_{t_i}^{\text{failure}}
&\approx q_{(i)}(t_i)
\\
&= \blue{\haz_0(t_i)\,\Delta t}\;\red{\hazfactor(\vx_{(i)})}
\\
&= \Pr\sb{(i) \text{ fails at } t_i \mid R(t_i)}.
\ea
$$ {#eq-ph-fail-interval-approx}

**Split each event factor into which-subject and whether-a-failure.**
Apply the chain rule
$\Pr(A \mid C) = \Pr(A \mid B \cap C)\,\Pr(B \mid C)$
with

- $A$ = "subject $(i)$ fails at $t_i$",
- $B$ = "exactly one failure occurs at $t_i$",
- $C$ = "the risk set at $t_i$ is $R(t_i)$".

Under the no-ties assumption $A \subset B$ (subject $(i)$ failing at
$t_i$ is automatically a one-failure event), so the rule is valid and
splits the event factor @eq-ph-fail-interval-approx into a
*which-subject-fails* factor and a *whether-a-failure-occurs* factor:

$$
\ba
\underbrace{\Pr\sb{(i) \text{ fails at } t_i \mid R(t_i)}}
          _{\approx\ q_{(i)}(t_i)\ =\ \blue{\haz_0(t_i)\,\Delta t}\,\red{\hazfactor(\vx_{(i)})}}
&= \underbrace{\Pr\sb{(i) \text{ fails} \mid 1 \text{ failure at } t_i,\ R(t_i)}}
             _{\text{which subject fails}}
\\
&\quad \times\
\underbrace{\Pr\sb{1 \text{ failure at } t_i \mid R(t_i)}}
          _{\text{whether a failure occurs}}.
\ea
$$ {#eq-ph-chain-rule}

**The whether-a-failure-occurs factor.**
Treating subjects' failure events as conditionally independent given
$R(t_i)$ (the Cox-model assumption used throughout;
see [@klein2003survival, §8.3]),
the probability of exactly one failure in $[t_i, t_i + \Delta t)$
is, by inclusion-exclusion,

$$
\Pr\sb{1 \text{ failure at } t_i \mid R(t_i)}
= \sum_{k \in R(t_i)}
    \Pr\sb{k \text{ fails} \mid R(t_i)}
    \prod_{m \in R(t_i),\, m \ne k} \Pr\sb{m \text{ survives} \mid R(t_i)}.
$$

By @eq-ph-single-subj,
$\Pr\sb{m \text{ fails} \mid R(t_i)} \approx q_m(t_i) = O(\Delta t)$,
so each product $\prod_{m \ne k} \Pr\sb{m \text{ survives} \mid R(t_i)} = \prod_{m \ne k}(1 - O(\Delta t)) = 1 - O(\Delta t)$,
and dropping $O(\Delta t^2)$ cross-terms (each pair of co-failures has
probability $O(\Delta t^2)$) leaves the sum of the single-subject pieces.
To pull the shared baseline factor out of that sum, define the
**risk-set score total**

$$
S_i \eqdef \sum_{k \in R(t_i)} \hazfactor(\vx_k),
$$ {#eq-ph-risk-set-score}

the total of the subject-specific hazard ratios $\red{\hazfactor(\vx_k)}$
over everyone at risk at $t_i$.
Then the shared $\blue{\haz_0(t_i)\,\Delta t}$ factors out of the sum:

$$
\ba
\Pr\sb{1 \text{ failure at } t_i \mid R(t_i)}
&\approx
\sum_{k \in R(t_i)} q_k(t_i)
\\
&=
\blue{\haz_0(t_i)\,\Delta t}\sum_{k \in R(t_i)} \red{\hazfactor(\vx_k)}
\\
&=
\blue{\haz_0(t_i)\,\Delta t}\;\red{S_i}.
\ea
$$ {#eq-ph-one-failure}

**The which-subject-fails factor.**
Solving @eq-ph-chain-rule for the conditional probability and
substituting the numerator $q_{(i)}(t_i)$ from @eq-ph-single-subj and the
denominator $\blue{\haz_0(t_i)\,\Delta t}\,\red{S_i}$ from
@eq-ph-one-failure, the shared $\blue{\haz_0(t_i)\,\Delta t}$ factor
cancels between numerator and denominator:

$$
\ba
\Pr\sb{(i) \text{ fails} \mid 1 \text{ failure at } t_i,\ R(t_i)}
&= \frac{\Pr\sb{(i) \text{ fails at } t_i \mid R(t_i)}}
         {\Pr\sb{1 \text{ failure at } t_i \mid R(t_i)}}
\\
&\approx \frac{q_{(i)}(t_i)}{\blue{\haz_0(t_i)\,\Delta t}\;\red{S_i}}
\\
&= \frac{\blue{\haz_0(t_i)\,\Delta t}\;\red{\hazfactor(\vx_{(i)})}}
         {\blue{\haz_0(t_i)\,\Delta t}\;\red{S_i}}
\\
&= \frac{\red{\hazfactor(\vx_{(i)})}}{\red{S_i}}
= \Lik^*_i(\b).
\ea
$$ {#eq-ph-which-subject}

The $\approx$ inherits the first-order hazard approximation
$\Pr\{T_j \in [t_i, t_i + \Delta t) \mid T_j \ge t_i\} \approx \haz(t_i \mid \vx_j)\,\Delta t$
(the *conditional* probability — the hazard's defining quantity, given
the subject has survived to $t_i$).
The final $=$ is exact: the $\blue{\haz_0(t_i)\,\Delta t}$ factors cancel
algebraically, leaving a ratio that is independent of $\Delta t$
altogether, so the $\Delta t \to 0$ limit is trivial and the
which-subject factor equals the partial-likelihood contribution
$\Lik^*_i(\b)$ of @def-ph-partial-lik.

**Factor the full likelihood.**
Return to the interval product @eq-ph-lik-by-interval and separate the
$K$ failure intervals from the rest.
Using @eq-ph-fail-interval-approx and then @eq-ph-chain-rule on each
failure interval,

$$
\ba
\Lik(\b, \haz_0)
&\approx
\paren{\prod_{i=1}^{K} \Lik_{t_i}^{\text{failure}}}
\paren{\prod_{\tau\,:\,\text{no failure}} \Lik_\tau^{\text{no failure}}}
\\
&= \paren{\prod_{i=1}^{K}
   \Pr\sb{(i) \text{ fails} \mid 1 \text{ failure at } t_i,\ R(t_i)}\,
   \Pr\sb{1 \text{ failure at } t_i \mid R(t_i)}}
   \paren{\prod_{\tau\,:\,\text{no failure}} \Lik_\tau^{\text{no failure}}}
\\
&\approx
\underbrace{\paren{\prod_{i=1}^{K} \frac{\red{\hazfactor(\vx_{(i)})}}{\red{S_i}}}}
          _{\Lik^*(\b)\ \text{(kept)}}
\;\times\;
\underbrace{
\sb{\prod_{i=1}^{K} \blue{\haz_0(t_i)\,\Delta t}\;\red{S_i}}
\sb{\prod_{\tau\,:\,\text{no failure}} \prod_{m \in R(\tau)} \paren{1 - q_m(\tau)}}}
          _{\mathcal{B}(\b,\,\haz_0)\ \text{(discarded)}}.
\ea
$$ {#eq-ph-factored-lik}

Under the no-ties assumption each event time has $d_i = 1$
(where $d_i$ is the number of events at distinct event time $t_i$),
so the kept
product $\prod_{i=1}^{K}(\cdots) = \prod_{\{i:\,d_i=1\}}(\cdots)$ matches
the form of $\Lik^*(\b)$ in @def-ph-partial-lik.

The partial likelihood keeps the first product, $\Lik^*(\b)$, and drops
the second, $\mathcal{B}(\b, \haz_0)$.
The discarded factor $\mathcal{B}(\b, \haz_0)$ collects both the
whether-a-failure-occurs factors and the entire between-event survival
background, and it is the only place the baseline hazard
$\blue{\haz_0(\cdot)}$ appears — equivalently, the shared
$\blue{\haz_0(t_i)\,\Delta t}$ cancels from each kept factor in
@eq-ph-which-subject — which is why maximizing $\Lik^*(\b)$ alone frees
the estimator from $\haz_0(\cdot)$.
The discarded factor is **not** free of $\b$, however:
it still depends on $\b$ through the risk-set score totals $\red{S_i}$
and the survival background.
Dropping it therefore sacrifices the information about $\b$ carried by
*when* (and within how large a risk set) failures occur,
retaining only the information in *which* subject fails at each event time.

The factorization in @eq-ph-factored-lik is a *construction* rather than
an exact decomposition of the joint probability: the factors are not
formally independent (the risk set $R(t_{i+1})$ depends on who failed
and was censored before $t_{i+1}$, and the discretization is exact only
in the $\Delta t \to 0$ limit),
but Cox showed that treating $\Lik^*(\b)$ as a likelihood — i.e.,
maximizing it as a function of $\b$ — yields estimators with the
usual large-sample properties (consistency, asymptotic normality,
the standard score and information identities;
see also [@klein2003survival, §8.3, Theoretical Note 1]).
Under noninformative censoring, each kept factor depends on the observed
history only through $R(t_i)$ and $\{\vx_j\}_{j \in R(t_i)}$, and
@eq-ph-which-subject gives exactly the
partial likelihood $\Lik^*(\b)$ of @def-ph-partial-lik.

:::
